settings.title: A Low-Maintenance Front Garden in Gravel and Grasses
settings.summary: We replaced a tired front lawn with a gravel garden that needs almost no upkeep.
settings.description:
Margaret wanted to stop mowing a scrappy front lawn that never looked good and turned to mud by the gate. The grass struggled in a dry, sunny spot close to the road, and the constant car dust did it no favours either.

The approach

Rather than try to keep grass alive where it clearly did not want to grow, we changed the planting to suit the conditions. We dug out the old turf, laid a permeable membrane, and topped it with angular gravel that locks together underfoot and lets rain through.

Into that we planted drifts of stipa, salvia, and verbena, all of which take the heat, the dry soil, and the road dust without complaint. We grouped them in repeated blocks so the garden reads as a considered scheme rather than a scattering of single plants.

How it performs

The gravel drains freely after rain, so there is no more mud at the gate. Through summer the salvia and verbena keep the bees on it from morning onwards, and the stipa catches any breeze and adds movement to what was a flat, tired patch.

  • Old lawn removed and replaced with a permeable gravel base
  • Drought-tolerant planting of stipa, salvia, and verbena
  • Free-draining surface that no longer turns to mud

The upkeep is now close to nothing. Margaret cuts everything back once in late winter and otherwise leaves the garden to look after itself, which was the whole point of the change.
